mappings.rationaleAI: Industrial, product, and cultural design addresses the nature and scope of decorative and functional design (A). Companies like Nokia, Ford, and Lego in product design address industrial design as an art (C.2.a) and diverse kinds of industrial design by function (C.2.b).
Fashion spanning haute couture to ready-to-wear addresses dress design (C.1) and dress accessories (C.1.a). Textile production connects to fabrics (B.2).
Letterforms and typeface design address calligraphy and lettering (C.4.a). Printing through digital media connects to typography and printing design (C.4.b).
Advertising formats including print, outdoor, and internet campaigns directly address advertising art and design (C.4.c).
Brand identity and media strategy address advertising art and design (C.4.c). Visual markers and logos connect to signs and symbols for identification (C.4.d).
Writing systems from cuneiform through calligraphic traditions address handwriting systems, calligraphy, and lettering (C.4.a).
Egyptian hieroglyphs as monumental art and pictographic script address handwriting systems and styles (C.4.a).
Metals, ceramics, and polymers address decorative materials (B) and metals (B.5). Cement connects to plaster, cement, and concrete (B.9.a). Polymers address plastics and synthetic materials (B.9.b).
Architecture as art form addresses interior design as an art (C.3.a). Form, function, and cultural meaning connect to integration of interior design with architecture (C.3.b).
Modernism transforming art, architecture, and design addresses the nature and scope of decorative and functional design (A).
Philosophy of beauty and aesthetic experience beyond fine arts addresses the nature and scope of decorative and functional design (A).
Classical temple design, sculpture, and material culture address stone as a decorative material (B.7).
Material culture and ethnographic study address the nature of decorative arts (A). Non-Western craft production connects to plant fibres, reeds, and baskets (B.10.b).
Prehistoric material evidence including tools from organic materials connects to shell, horn, bone, ivory, and related materials (B.10.d).
Mineral families connect to gems (B.3) as precious stones are mineral formations. Mineralogy also addresses stone (B.7).
Industrial chemistry including materials for clothing and furnishings connects to plastics and synthetic materials (B.9.b).

Egyptian decorative objects address the nature of decorative arts (A). Stone sculpture connects to stone (B.7). Metal objects connect to metals (B.5). Cosmetics in Egyptian society relate to body decoration (C.1.c).
Renaissance decorative arts address the nature of decorative arts (A). Art in homes and civic spaces connects to interior design (C.3). Metalwork addresses metals (B.5).
